h1: Calculate Soil pH Correction & Agricultural Lime Dosage
description: Calculate how much agricultural lime to add for soil pH correction. Free & fast calculator.
intro: Successful gardening and farming depends on understanding soil chemistry, plant needs, and proper timing. Soil pH directly affects nutrient availability and plant growth. Use this free calculator to determine exactly how much agricultural lime you need to adjust your soil pH to optimal levels. Our calculations are current for 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep your gardening knowledge up-to-date.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **kg/m² = (target_pH − current_pH) × 0.5**. Simply enter your current soil pH, target pH, and garden area to calculate the exact amount of agricultural lime needed.
useCases: Agronomists and soil scientists validating recommendations

example.title: Example calculation
steps: Current pH 5.5 → Target pH 6.5, Area 50 m²

explanation:
## Soil Chemistry for Gardeners

Soil pH measures acidity or alkalinity on a scale of 0–14. Most vegetables thrive in slightly acidic to neutral soil (pH 6–7). Agricultural lime (calcium carbonate) raises pH gradually over 2–3 months and is typically applied in fall or winter.

## How the Calculation Works

This calculator uses the standard lime requirement formula based on your soil's current pH, target pH, and garden area. It alerts you when values fall outside typical ranges for safe soil amendment.

## Important Notes

This calculation is a reference guide for planning. For critical decisions involving significant land, crops, or finances, consult with a local agronomist or soil specialist. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What does agricultural lime do for soil?
faq.a: Agricultural lime (calcium carbonate) raises soil pH gradually. It's the standard amendment for correcting acidic soils and improving nutrient availability to plants.
Fall or winter is ideal—lime takes 2–3 months to fully react with soil, allowing time for pH adjustment before spring planting.

faq.q: How do I lower soil pH instead?
faq.a: Use elemental sulfur or aluminum sulfate. These lower pH but work more slowly than lime (4–6 months). Acidic mulches like pine needles also help gradually.

faq.q: How do I test my soil pH?
faq.a: pH test strips (~$10) or a digital pH meter (~$30–50) both work. Most reliable: send a soil sample to your local agricultural extension office for professional analysis.

faq.q: What's the ideal soil pH for vegetables?
faq.a: Most vegetables prefer pH 6–7 (slightly acidic to neutral). Acid-loving plants like blueberries thrive at pH 4.5–5.5; potatoes do best around pH 5.5.

faq.q: Why is soil pH so important?
faq.a: pH controls nutrient availability. In very acidic soil, essential nutrients lock up. In alkaline soil, iron and manganese become unavailable, causing plant deficiencies.
Yes—all our calculators are completely free, require no registration, and have no ads. We built them as a resource for gardeners and farmers.
These use standardized agronomy formulas. Results are reliable for planning, but soil varies widely. For critical applications, get a professional soil test from your extension office.

faq.q: Do you store or share my data?
faq.a: No. All calculations run in your browser. We never send your data to any server or store it anywhere.
